Stonewall Partners* (Issue 6), the Appointment Data reveals a substantially larger body of concurrent and prior engagements with the parties' counsel than the Disclosure Statement reflects. The "complete list" certification is therefore inaccurate.

*(Note on the source data: The Entity Memo's narrative reports Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP as appearing in 4 completed matters (\~\$280,000; \~13.2% of income), whereas the Appointment Data shows 5 matters (\~\$410,000; \~19.3%). The variance arises from the dual-firm matter* Westbrook Partners v. Arcadian Ventures\* (01-22-0002134), in which both Barrow & Thistlewood LLP and Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP appeared; the Appointment Data counts each firm's appearances independently, while the Entity Memo's narrative appears to count that matter under Barrow & Thistlewood LLP only. Both sources agree on the Barrow & Thistlewood LLP figures (9 matters; \~\$1,020,000; \~48% of income; 77.8% prevailing rate) and on Fielding Marsh's 25% prevailing rate. The variance does not affect the conclusions below.)\*

-   **Governing standard:** Rule R-17(a)(iii) (prior or pending cases with a party's counsel); Rule R-17(d) (consequences of incomplete disclosure).
-   **Direction of apparent partiality:** Toward the Respondent (the understatement disproportionately concerns Barrow & Thistlewood LLP, the Respondent's counsel).
-   **Assessment:** **Strong.** The certification of a "complete list" is directly contradicted by the Appointment Data. The omission of multiple matters --- including one in which *both* parties' counsel appeared --- is a material disclosure deficiency.

#### Issue 9 --- Economic-dependency and outcome-asymmetry concern arising from the repeat-appointment pattern

Standing alone, repeat appointments are common in the arbitration community and are not, without more, disqualifying; the AAA Rules contemplate that parties and counsel will appear before experienced arbitrators more than once. Here, however, the repeat-appointment pattern has features that, taken together with the non-disclosure of its full extent, give rise to justifiable doubt:

-   **Economic concentration.** An estimated **\~48% of the arbitrator's arbitration income** over the past 36 months is attributable to matters in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (the Respondent's counsel) appeared, and Barrow & Thistlewood LLP is involved in **3 of the arbitrator's 5 currently pending matters**, accounting for an estimated **\$560,000 (67.1%)** of pending fees (Appointment Data, Pending Matters and Fee Summary sheets).

-   **Outcome asymmetry.** The Barrow & Thistlewood LLP client prevailed in **7 of 9** completed matters (77.8%), while the Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP client prevailed in only **1 of 4** adjudicated matters (25%). While outcome data can reflect the merits of individual cases rather than bias, a persistent and substantial disparity --- combined with the arbitrator's significant economic dependence on one firm and the non-disclosure of the full extent of that relationship --- is a circumstance a reasonable party would consider relevant under Rule R-16.

-   **Selection asymmetry.** Whitmore (the Respondent) ranked the arbitrator **first**, while Cascade (the Claimant) ranked her **third** --- a selection outcome that, in light of the undisclosed pro-Respondent connections, takes on added significance.

-   **Governing standard:** Rule R-16 (justifiable doubt; all circumstances a reasonable person would consider relevant); Rule R-17(a)(ii)--(iii); FAA § 10(a)(2) (evident partiality).

-   **Direction of apparent partiality:** Toward the Respondent.

-   **Assessment:** **Moderate-to-strong when combined with Issues 6 and 8.** The economic-dependency and outcome-asymmetry data are not, in isolation, a standalone ground for disqualification, but they materially strengthen the challenge by supplying a concrete basis for the "justifiable doubt" that the non-disclosures independently raise. The arbitrator's failure to disclose the full extent of the repeat-appointment relationship (Issue 8) prevents the parties from evaluating this concern on a complete record.

### C. Disclosed Connections That Are Not, Themselves, Deficient (Context)

For completeness, the following were disclosed and are not, standing alone, deficient --- although several are rendered incomplete by the issues above:

-   The seven listed prior/pending arbitrations (Section III) --- disclosed, but understated (Issue 8) and missing the pending *Halcyon Biotech* matter (Issue 6).
-   Ellen Wu's advisory-board service at the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute (Section VI) --- disclosed, but incomplete because it omits Garrett T. Whitmore's Board of Trustees service at the same organization (Issue 1).
-   The Lakeview affiliation and the fact of referrals from both firms (Section VII) --- disclosed, but the financial/structural terms are omitted (Issue 7), and the Lakeview-hosted CLE co-paneling with Mr. Hargrave is omitted (Issue 4).
-   The arbitrator's subject-matter experience in technology licensing, IP valuation, and fiduciary-duty claims in the private-equity context (Section VIII) --- disclosed and not itself problematic; however, the arbitrator's expressed views on these very topics at the undisclosed October 14, 2023 CLE are a separate disclosure concern under R-17(a)(iv) (Issue 4).
-   The fee arrangement (\$850/hour; \~\$340,000 estimated; \~\$170,000 per party) (Section IX) --- disclosed and not itself a ground for challenge; the broader income-concentration concern is addressed at Issue 9.

## V. Procedural Considerations --- Challenge Deadline, Waiver, and Continuing Duty

### A. The Challenge Deadline

The arbitrator states in Section X of the Disclosure Statement that, pursuant to Rule R-18, the parties have until **June 24, 2024** --- seven calendar days from the date of the disclosure --- to raise an objection. Rule R-18(a) measures the seven-day period from the later of (i) communication of the appointment (June 10, 2024) or (ii) the date the challenging party knew or should have known of the circumstances giving rise to the challenge. Because the disclosure was communicated on June 17, 2024, June 24, 2024 is the operative deadline for any challenge based on facts disclosed *in* the Disclosure Statement.

### B. The Waiver Exception for Newly Discovered Grounds

Several of the grounds identified above --- particularly Issues 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 --- concern facts that were **not disclosed** and that a party could not have learned from the Disclosure Statement itself. They were surfaced only through independent public-records research (the Entity Memo, dated June 19, 2024, and the Appointment Data). Rule R-18(f) provides that a party is **not** deemed to have waived its right to challenge "based on grounds that were not and could not reasonably have been known at the time of the deadline." Accordingly, a challenge based on the undisclosed connections would be timely if made within seven calendar days of the date the challenging party knew or should have known of them. For a party in possession of the Entity Memo and Appointment Data, that period would run from approximately June 19--20, 2024. Rule R-18(e) further preserves the right to seek vacatur under FAA § 10(a)(2) or applicable state law, including on grounds of evident partiality not previously known and subject to waiver.

### C. The Arbitrator's Continuing Duty to Disclose

Rule R-17(b) imposes a **continuing** obligation to disclose new information "as soon as practicable," without awaiting inquiry. The arbitrator acknowledges this duty in Sections I and X of the Disclosure Statement. Even if the initial disclosure was deficient, the arbitrator is now on notice (through the Entity Memo and Appointment Data, and through any party inquiry) of facts warranting disclosure and should promptly supplement. A failure to supplement after the facts are brought to her attention would itself strengthen any subsequent challenge or vacatur motion.

  2           Spouse (Dr. Alan Fairhaven) co-authored paper with Dr. Rita Peshkov (Claimant's key expert witness); topic directly relevant                                                              No                  R-17(a)(ii); R-16                          Claimant                              Strong

  3           Affirmative misstatement that spouse has "no ... professional relationship with ... any of \[the parties'\] officers, directors, or employees" (Peshkov is a Cascade officer)             No (misstatement)   R-17(a)(ii); R-17(d); FAA § 10(a)(2)       Claimant / credibility                Strong

  4           CLE co-panelist with Dominic Hargrave (Respondent's lead counsel) on "Arbitrating Complex IP Licensing Disputes" (Oct. 14, 2023); topic directly germane; hosted at Lakeview              No                  R-17(a)(ii); R-17(a)(iv)                   Respondent                            Strong

  5           Indirect financial interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (owner of Volaris) via Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund (2.3% LP interest); "no direct equity positions" omits indirect exposure   No                  R-17(a)(i); R-16; IBA Guidelines           Respondent                            Strong (warrants inquiry)

  6           Undisclosed pending arbitration *Halcyon Biotech v. Stonewall Partners* (B&T counsel)                                                                                                     No                  R-17(a)(iii)                               Respondent                            Moderate--strong

  7           Lakeview affiliation: financial/structural terms not disclosed; Lakeview hosted the CLE with Hargrave                                                                                     Partial             R-17(a)(ii); R-16                          Respondent (referral concentration)   Moderate

  8           "Complete list" certification inaccurate; \~6--7 additional completed matters and 1 additional pending matter involving B&T/FM not disclosed                                              No (understated)    R-17(a)(iii); R-17(d)                      Respondent                            Strong

  9           Economic dependency (\~48% of income from B&T; 67% of pending fees) and outcome asymmetry (B&T 77.8% vs. FM 25% prevail rates), combined with non-disclosure                              No (extent)         R-16; R-17(a)(ii)--(iii); FAA § 10(a)(2)   Respondent                            Moderate--strong (cumulative)
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Net assessment.** The Disclosure Statement contains **five wholly undisclosed connections** (Issues 1, 2, 4, 5, 6), **one affirmative misstatement** (Issue 3), **one incomplete disclosure** (Issue 7), and **one inaccurate certification** with an associated economic-dependency/outcome-asymmetry concern (Issues 8--9). The undisclosed connections create appearances of partiality in **both** directions: Issues 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 tend toward the Respondent; Issues 2 and 3 tend toward the Claimant. The pattern of non-disclosure across multiple categories --- financial interest, family/witness relationships, professional relationships with counsel and a party principal, and prior/pending matters --- is itself a basis for justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator's independence and the diligence of her disclosure, independent of the direction of any individual connection.

## VII. Recommendation

1.  **Request a comprehensive supplemental disclosure.** Before deciding whether to file a formal challenge, the arbitrator should be asked (through the AAA, with notice to all parties) to supplement her disclosure to address, specifically and in writing: (a) Garrett T. Whitmore's service on the Board of Trustees of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute and the nature of any interaction with the arbitrator through that organization (Issue 1); (b) the co-authorship between Dr. Alan Fairhaven and Dr. Rita Peshkov, the scope and timing of that collaboration, and any ongoing relationship, and to correct the affirmative statement in Section V (Issues 2--3); (c) the October 14, 2023 CLE co-paneling with Dominic Hargrave, including any views the arbitrator expressed on IP-licensing valuation or arbitrator selection in technology cases (Issue 4); (d) whether the arbitrator's portfolio holds any allocation to the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund or any other vehicle with exposure to Whitmore Fund II LP, Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, Volaris, or Cascade, directly or indirectly (Issue 5); (e) the omitted pending matter *Halcyon Biotech v. Stonewall Partners* and a complete list of all prior and pending matters involving Barrow & Thistlewood LLP or Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP (Issues 6, 8); and (f) the financial and structural terms of the Lakeview affiliation (Issue 7). This request invokes the arbitrator's continuing duty under Rule R-17(b) and creates a record for any later challenge or vacatur motion.

2.  **Evaluate the response; prepare a formal challenge if warranted.** If the supplemental disclosure confirms the connections identified above (or if the arbitrator declines to supplement), a formal challenge under Rule R-18 should be prepared. The challenge must state the grounds "with specificity," in writing to the AAA with a copy to the other party. Given the breadth and materiality of the non-disclosures --- particularly the indirect financial interest in the Respondent's fund (Issue 5), the spouse--witness co-authorship and affirmative misstatement (Issues 2--3), the CLE co-paneling with the Respondent's lead counsel on the exact subject matter (Issue 4), the shared organizational service with the Respondent's principal (Issue 1), and the inaccurate "complete list" certification together with the economic-dependency and outcome-asymmetry data (Issues 6, 8, 9) --- there are substantial grounds to support a challenge on the basis of justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator's impartiality and independence.

3.  **Mind the deadline and the waiver exception.** Any challenge based on facts already disclosed in the Disclosure Statement must be filed by **June 24, 2024**. Challenges based on the newly discovered, undisclosed connections (Issues 1--6) are preserved under Rule R-18(f) and should be filed within seven calendar days of the date the grounds became known. The supplemental-disclosure request (Recommendation 1) should be made immediately and in any event before the June 24 deadline, so that the arbitrator's response (or failure to respond) can inform the challenge decision and the record.

4.  **Preserve FAA § 10(a)(2) rights.** Regardless of the AAA's administrative ruling on any challenge, the parties should preserve their rights to seek vacatur of any resulting award on grounds of evident partiality under FAA § 10(a)(2), particularly as to any non-disclosure that is not cured by supplemental disclosure. Rule R-18(e) expressly preserves these rights, and Rule R-17(d) ties disclosure deficiencies to vacatur exposure.

5.  **Consider the direction of the challenge.** The strongest challenge grounds (Issues 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9) support a challenge by the **Claimant** (Cascade / Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP) on the theory that the arbitrator's undisclosed connections create an appearance of partiality toward the Respondent. The spouse--witness co-authorship and the affirmative misstatement (Issues 2--3) support a challenge by the **Respondent** (Whitmore / Barrow & Thistlewood LLP) on the theory of an appearance of partiality toward the Claimant. Because the non-disclosures run in both directions, either party has a colorable basis to challenge; the Respondent's own diligence (the Entity Memo and Appointment Data) has surfaced grounds that primarily support a Claimant challenge, which the Respondent should weigh in deciding whether to support the arbitrator's continued service, to join or anticipate a challenge, or to seek a mutually acceptable replacement under Rule R-19.

*This memorandum is privileged and confidential, prepared in anticipation of litigation and for the purpose of providing legal advice. It is protected by the attorney-client privilege and the work-product doctrine. Factual assertions are drawn from the source documents identified in Section I; no facts have been supplied beyond the record.*

## Deliverable

**`output/arbitrator-disclosure-issues-memo.docx`** — validated against the ECMA-376 WordprocessingML schema.

The memo identifies **9 distinct grounds for challenge**, each mapped to the governing standard (AAA R-16/R-17(a)(i)–(iv)/R-17(b)/(d), R-18(a)/(e)/(f), FAA § 10(a)(2), the AAA/ABA Code of Ethics, and the IBA Guidelines), with the direction of apparent partiality and a strength assessment for each:

2. Undisclosed spouse–witness co-authorship (Dr. Alan Fairhaven & Dr. Rita Peshkov, Sept. 2022)

8. Inaccurate "complete list" certification (~6–7 additional undisclosed matters)
9. Economic-dependency / outcome-asymmetry concern (~48% of income from B&T; 77.8% vs. 25% prevail rates)

It also covers the procedural posture (June 24 deadline, the R-18(f) waiver exception for newly discovered grounds, the continuing duty to disclose), a consolidated summary table, and a five-part recommendation. A key analytical point: the non-disclosures run in both directions, so either party has a colorable challenge, with the strongest grounds supporting a Claimant challenge.

3. CHECK EACH REQUIRED OUTPUT / DIMENSION: confirm every deliverable the task asked for exists, and that any required comparison, matrix, or dimension (e.g. every jurisdiction, every party, every provision, every document in the file) is covered — do not drop a category because it seemed minor.

result:
**[BARROW & THISTLEWOOD LLP]{.underline}**

**[Internal Research Memorandum]{.underline}**

**TO:** Dominic Hargrave, Partner, Barrow & Thistlewood LLP

**FROM:** Catherine M. Aldridge, Associate, Barrow & Thistlewood LLP

**DATE:** June 19, 2024

**RE:** Entity and Relationship Profiles --- AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217, *Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. v. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC*

**CLASSIFICATION:** Attorney-Client Privileged / Attorney Work Product

This memorandum compiles publicly available information regarding all parties, counsel, the arbitrator, and their known interconnections in connection with the above-referenced arbitration. Information was gathered from public corporate filings, SEC databases (including EDGAR, Form ADV, and Form D filings), AAA arbitrator directories, academic publication databases, bar association records, CLE event archives, nonprofit tax filings (IRS Form 990), professional networking platforms, and public arbitration award databases. The memorandum is organized by entity or individual, with subsections addressing background facts and cross-references to identified relationships with other parties, counsel, and the arbitrator. No privileged or confidential sources were used in the preparation of this memorandum. All factual assertions herein are drawn from publicly accessible records. This memorandum does not provide legal analysis or conclusions; it is intended to serve as a factual foundation for the legal team\'s evaluation of the arbitrator\'s disclosures and any potential challenge under applicable AAA rules.

**[SECTION I: PARTIES TO THE ARBITRATION]{.underline}**

**1.1 Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (Respondent)**

Whitmore Capital Partners LLC is a Delaware limited liability company organized for the purpose of serving as a private equity fund manager. The company maintains its principal offices at 200 Harborview Drive, Suite 1400, Boston, Massachusetts 02110. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC was founded in 2009 by Garrett T. Whitmore, who continues to serve as Founder and Managing Partner. The firm manages approximately \$3.2 billion in assets under management across three fund vintages: Whitmore Fund I LP, Whitmore Fund II LP, and Whitmore Fund III LP. Each fund is structured as a separate Delaware limited partnership, with Whitmore Capital Partners LLC serving as the general partner of each fund. The fund structure is relevant to this arbitration because the disputed transaction involves a cross-fund transfer of economic value between portfolio companies held by different Whitmore funds.

**Whitmore Fund II LP.** Whitmore Fund II LP is a Delaware limited partnership. Its portfolio companies include Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, which is discussed in greater detail in Section 1.3 below. Whitmore Fund II LP is relevant to this arbitration because the disputed below-market technology license directly benefits Volaris, a Fund II portfolio company. The economic interests of Fund II and its limited partners are therefore directly implicated by the outcome of this proceeding. As discussed further in Section III.4 below, certain publicly available fund filings indicate that third-party investment vehicles hold limited partner interests in Whitmore Fund II LP.

**Whitmore Fund III LP.** Whitmore Fund III LP is a Delaware limited partnership. On January 8, 2019, Whitmore Fund III LP acquired a 62% controlling stake in Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. for approximately \$118 million. This acquisition is the foundation of Whitmore Capital Partners LLC\'s control over Cascade and forms the basis for the fiduciary duty claims asserted in this arbitration. The minority shareholders of Cascade allege that Whitmore, exercising control through Fund III\'s majority ownership position, directed Cascade to enter into a below-market technology license agreement that benefited a Fund II portfolio company (Volaris) at the expense of Cascade and its minority shareholders.

**Garrett T. Whitmore --- Additional Affiliations.** In addition to his role as Founder and Managing Partner of Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, publicly available records indicate that Garrett T. Whitmore holds a position with a nonprofit professional organization relevant to other participants in this arbitration. Per publicly available IRS Form 990 filings for the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute (employer identification number on file), Garrett T. Whitmore is listed as a member of the **Board of Trustees** of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute. His name appears on the Institute\'s most recently filed Form 990, covering fiscal year 2023, in the capacity of Trustee. The Board of Trustees is the governing body of the Institute, responsible for strategic direction, budgetary oversight, and leadership appointments. The Board of Trustees is formally distinct from the Institute\'s advisory board. The significance of this affiliation is discussed in Section III.3 below, which addresses the arbitrator\'s service on the advisory board of the same organization.

**1.2 Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. (Claimant)**

Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. is a Delaware corporation headquartered at 8500 Industrial Parkway, Building C, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55402. The company is a manufacturer of industrial heat-exchange systems and holds 14 patents related to advanced thermal management technology, including the proprietary \"ThermalCore\" heat-exchange technology that is at the center of this arbitration. Cascade\'s ThermalCore technology is described in the arbitration demand as a next-generation phase-change heat-exchange system with applications in heavy industrial manufacturing environments. The technology represents a significant portion of Cascade\'s intellectual property portfolio and, per the claimant\'s allegations, a substantial component of the company\'s enterprise value.

Whitmore Fund III LP holds a 62% controlling interest in Cascade. Minority shareholders collectively hold the remaining 38% of outstanding shares. The arbitration was initiated as a derivative action by minority shareholders alleging that Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, acting through its control of Cascade\'s board, caused Cascade to enter into a below-market technology license agreement with Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, a portfolio company of a different Whitmore fund (Fund II), to the detriment of Cascade and its minority shareholders.

**Key Personnel:**

**Janet Okafor** --- Ms. Okafor is a member of Cascade\'s board of directors and a minority shareholder holding approximately 11.4% of the outstanding shares of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. She is named as the claimant representative in the arbitration demand filed on March 15, 2024. Ms. Okafor led the derivative action brought by the minority shareholders and has been identified as a principal witness for the claimant on matters relating to corporate governance, board deliberations, and the circumstances surrounding the approval of the Volaris license agreement.

**Dr. Rita Peshkov** --- Dr. Peshkov serves as Vice President of Research & Development at Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. She has been with the company since 2017 and oversaw the development of the ThermalCore technology that is the subject of the disputed license. The arbitration demand, filed March 15, 2024, identifies Dr. Peshkov as an anticipated key expert witness for the claimant on matters including the technical specifications, proprietary nature, and competitive value of the licensed technology. Dr. Peshkov holds a Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering and has an academic publication record in the field of thermal engineering. Her publication history is addressed further in Section III.2 below, in connection with the arbitrator\'s spouse.

**1.3 Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC**

Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC is a Delaware limited liability company headquartered at 1200 Commerce Boulevard, Charlotte, North Carolina 28202. The company is an industrial manufacturer and operates as a portfolio company of Whitmore Fund II LP. The current Chief Executive Officer of Volaris is Thomas Kinnear.

Volaris is the recipient of the contested below-market technology license from Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. for Cascade\'s proprietary ThermalCore heat-exchange technology. The license agreement was executed on March 1, 2023, at a rate of \$2.8 million per year for a 7-year term. An independent valuation prepared by Ridgeline Advisory Group, completed on November 15, 2023, concluded that the fair market value of the ThermalCore technology license was \$9.5 million per year. The alleged annual differential between the license rate and fair market value is therefore \$6.7 million (\$9.5 million minus \$2.8 million), yielding total alleged damages of \$46.9 million over the 7-year term of the license (\$6.7 million multiplied by 7 years).

The cross-fund nature of this transaction is central to the self-dealing allegations at issue in this arbitration. Volaris is owned by Whitmore Fund II LP, not by Whitmore Fund III LP, which is the fund that holds the 62% controlling stake in Cascade. The claimant alleges that Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, as general partner of both Fund II and Fund III, directed a Fund III portfolio company (Cascade) to provide a below-market license to a Fund II portfolio company (Volaris), thereby transferring economic value from Fund III and its co-investors (including Cascade\'s minority shareholders) to Fund II and its limited partners. This cross-fund structure is also relevant to the arbitrator\'s financial interests, as discussed in Section III.4 below.

**[SECTION II: COUNSEL]{.underline}**

**2.1 Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (Respondent\'s Counsel)**

Barrow & Thistlewood LLP is a national law firm headquartered at One Federal Plaza, 38th Floor, Boston, Massachusetts 02109. The firm has approximately 420 attorneys across multiple offices. Barrow & Thistlewood represents Whitmore Capital Partners LLC in this arbitration.

**Dominic Hargrave** --- Mr. Hargrave is the lead partner on this matter. He is a senior partner in the firm\'s litigation and arbitration practice group, based in the Boston office. Mr. Hargrave is admitted to practice in Massachusetts and has pro hac vice applications on file for this arbitration in Minnesota. His professional activities include speaking engagements at continuing legal education events related to arbitration and intellectual property disputes. As discussed in Section III.6 below, Mr. Hargrave participated as a co-panelist with the arbitrator at a CLE event on October 14, 2023, on a topic directly germane to the subject matter of this arbitration.

**Ellen Wu** --- Ms. Wu is a Senior Partner at Barrow & Thistlewood LLP and serves as the head of the firm\'s national arbitration practice. She is based in the firm\'s Boston office. Ms. Wu serves on the **advisory board** of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute, a nonprofit professional organization focused on ethical standards in alternative dispute resolution. As discussed in Section III.3 below, the arbitrator in this matter also serves on the advisory board of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute. While Ms. Wu is not lead counsel on this matter, she supervises the firm\'s arbitration practice and is involved in arbitrator selection strategy for matters handled by the practice group, including this arbitration. The arbitrator\'s disclosure statement references Ms. Wu\'s advisory board service at the Institute.

**2.2 Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP (Claimant\'s Counsel)**

Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP is a regional litigation firm headquartered at 600 Washington Avenue South, Suite 2100, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55401. The firm has approximately 85 attorneys and maintains a focused practice in commercial litigation, shareholder disputes, and corporate governance matters. Fielding Marsh & Collier represents the Cascade minority shareholders in this arbitration.

**Priya Narayanan** --- Ms. Narayanan is a partner at Fielding Marsh & Collier and serves as lead counsel for the claimant in this arbitration. She specializes in shareholder disputes and corporate governance litigation and is based in the firm\'s Minneapolis office. Ms. Narayanan signed the arbitration demand filed on March 15, 2024.

**[SECTION III: THE ARBITRATOR --- HON. MARGARET R. FAIRHAVEN (RET.)]{.underline}**

**3.1 Background and Professional Profile**

Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.) is a retired Minnesota state court judge who served on the Hennepin County District Court bench from 2004 to 2020. Judge Fairhaven earned her J.D. from the University of Wisconsin Law School in 1989 and an LL.M. in Intellectual Property from the Franklin Pierce Center for Intellectual Property in 1994. Since her retirement from the bench in 2020, Judge Fairhaven has practiced as a full-time commercial arbitrator and mediator. She is currently affiliated as a neutral with the Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center, located at 750 Nicollet Mall, Suite 3200, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55402. She is listed on the American Arbitration Association\'s Large Complex Case panel and the AAA\'s Technology Case Management panel. Based on publicly available records, Judge Fairhaven has conducted approximately 85 arbitrations since her retirement in 2020, establishing her as a prominent commercial arbitrator in the Minneapolis market and in the national arbitration community.

**Appointment in This Matter.** The parties selected Judge Fairhaven as sole arbitrator on June 3, 2024. During the ranking process, Whitmore Capital Partners LLC ranked Judge Fairhaven as its first choice, and Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. ranked her third. The AAA confirmed Judge Fairhaven\'s appointment on June 10, 2024. Judge Fairhaven submitted her disclosure statement on June 17, 2024. Under the applicable AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules, the deadline for either party to challenge the arbitrator based on the disclosure statement is **June 24, 2024** --- seven days from the date the disclosure was submitted.

**Compensation.** Judge Fairhaven\'s posted hourly rate is \$850 per hour. Based on the anticipated scope of the proceedings --- which include 15 scheduled hearing days, substantial pre-hearing motion practice, and post-hearing briefing --- the estimated total arbitrator fees for this matter are approximately \$340,000. This estimate is derived from approximately 400 hours of anticipated work (15 hearing days at 8 hours per day, yielding 120 hearing hours, plus an estimated 280 hours of pre-hearing and post-hearing work; 400 hours multiplied by \$850 per hour equals \$340,000). Under the AAA\'s standard fee allocation, each party\'s estimated share of the arbitrator\'s fees is \$170,000.

**3.2 Family and Personal Connections**

**Dr. Alan Fairhaven** --- Dr. Alan Fairhaven is the husband of the arbitrator. He is a retired professor of mechanical engineering from the University of Minnesota, where he served on the faculty for over two decades. Dr. Fairhaven is based in Minneapolis. The arbitrator\'s disclosure statement identifies Dr. Alan Fairhaven as her spouse and notes his former faculty position at the University of Minnesota. The disclosure does not provide further detail regarding Dr. Alan Fairhaven\'s professional activities, research collaborations, or ongoing academic relationships.

**Publication Record --- Co-Authorship with Dr. Rita Peshkov.** A search of academic publication databases reveals that Dr. Alan Fairhaven co-authored a peer-reviewed paper with **Dr. Rita Peshkov**, who is Cascade Thermal Systems\' Vice President of Research & Development and an anticipated key expert witness for the claimant in this arbitration (see Section 1.2 above). The publication details are as follows:

> **Title:** \"Optimization of Phase-Change Heat Exchangers in High-Load Industrial Applications\"
>
> **Journal:** *Journal of Advanced Thermal Engineering*
>
> **Citation:** Vol. 42, Issue 3, September 2022
>
> **Authors:** Alan R. Fairhaven, Ph.D.; Rita S. Peshkov, Ph.D.

The paper addresses optimization techniques for phase-change heat exchangers in industrial settings, including design methodologies and performance modeling for high-load applications. This subject matter is directly relevant to Cascade\'s proprietary ThermalCore technology at issue in this arbitration, which is described in the arbitration demand as a phase-change heat-exchange system designed for heavy industrial manufacturing environments.

Co-authorship of a peer-reviewed journal article in a specialized academic field typically reflects a sustained professional collaboration between the authors. Such collaboration ordinarily involves joint research design, data collection and analysis, manuscript drafting, peer review response, and editorial revision --- a process that can extend over many months. It is reasonable to infer that a professional and possibly personal relationship exists between Dr. Alan Fairhaven and Dr. Peshkov arising from this research collaboration. The September 2022 publication date indicates that the research and writing process likely began well before that date, and the professional relationship may have continued beyond publication.

The arbitrator\'s disclosure statement identifies Dr. Alan Fairhaven as her husband and states his former position at the University of Minnesota. The disclosure statement does not reference the co-authored paper, does not identify any relationship between Dr. Alan Fairhaven and Dr. Rita Peshkov, and does not address any connection between the arbitrator\'s spouse and any witness or party representative in this arbitration.

**3.3 Professional Organization Affiliations --- Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute**

The arbitrator has disclosed in her June 17, 2024 disclosure statement that she has served on the **advisory board** of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute since 2021. The advisory board provides substantive guidance on programming, ethics standards, and professional development initiatives for the Institute. The arbitrator\'s disclosure also states that **Ellen Wu**, Senior Partner at Barrow & Thistlewood LLP and head of that firm\'s national arbitration practice, also serves on the advisory board of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute. The disclosure of Ms. Wu\'s advisory board service was noted in the context of the arbitrator\'s description of her professional affiliations.

**Additional Finding --- Garrett T. Whitmore\'s Involvement.** Public records --- specifically, the IRS Form 990 filings of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute --- reveal that **Garrett T. Whitmore**, Founder and Managing Partner of Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (the respondent in this arbitration), serves on the **Board of Trustees** of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute. As noted in Section 1.1 above, the Board of Trustees is the Institute\'s governing body, responsible for strategic direction, budgetary oversight, and appointments to leadership positions, including appointments to the advisory board. The advisory board, on which the arbitrator sits, provides substantive guidance on programming and ethics standards, and its members are appointed by or serve at the pleasure of the Board of Trustees.

While the Board of Trustees and the advisory board are formally distinct bodies within the Institute\'s governance structure, they serve the same relatively small nonprofit organization. The Institute\'s publicly available bylaws and posted meeting minutes indicate that both bodies attend the Institute\'s annual meetings, and that periodic joint sessions between the Board of Trustees and the advisory board are held to discuss programming priorities and organizational strategy. Given the small size of the organization and the overlapping meeting structures, it is reasonable to infer that the arbitrator and the respondent\'s principal, Garrett T. Whitmore, interact in the context of their shared service to this organization.

The arbitrator\'s disclosure statement does not reference Garrett T. Whitmore\'s role at the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute. The disclosure identifies the arbitrator\'s own advisory board service and Ellen Wu\'s advisory board service, but does not address whether any party or party principal serves in any capacity with the Institute.

**3.4 Investment and Financial Interests**

The arbitrator\'s June 17, 2024 disclosure statement indicates that her personal investment portfolio is managed by **Northbridge Wealth Advisors**, an investment management firm. The disclosure statement affirmatively states that the arbitrator holds \"no direct equity positions in any party to this arbitration.\" The disclosure does not further describe the composition of her investment portfolio, and it does not address indirect interests, fund-of-funds exposure, or the specific holdings of any pooled investment vehicle in which she may participate.

**Additional Finding --- Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund.** A review of publicly available SEC filings and fund disclosure documents reveals that Northbridge Wealth Advisors manages a fund-of-funds product called the **Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund**. The Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund is a pooled investment vehicle that allocates capital across a diversified portfolio of private equity, venture capital, and alternative investment funds. Per the fund\'s most recent public filing, dated March 2024, the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund holds a **2.3% limited partner interest in Whitmore Fund II LP**.

As discussed in Section 1.3 above, Whitmore Fund II LP is the Delaware limited partnership that owns **Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC**, the direct beneficiary of the below-market technology license at the center of this arbitration. The economic outcome of this arbitration --- specifically, any award that results in the rescission, renegotiation, or repricing of the Volaris license agreement, or that imposes damages on Whitmore Capital Partners LLC --- has the potential to affect the value of Whitmore Fund II LP\'s portfolio and, by extension, the value of any limited partner interest in Fund II.

If the arbitrator\'s personal investment portfolio, which is managed by Northbridge Wealth Advisors, includes allocations to the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund --- a product managed by the same firm that manages her portfolio --- she may hold an **indirect financial interest** in Whitmore Fund II LP, and by extension, in the economic outcome of this arbitration. > Arbitrator\'s investment portfolio (managed by Northbridge Wealth Advisors)
>

>
> → Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund holds a 2.3% LP interest in Whitmore Fund II LP

The disclosure does not elaborate on the nature or volume of these referrals, or whether the referral relationships are material to Lakeview\'s business.

The public record does not clearly indicate whether the arbitrator\'s affiliation with Lakeview is structured as an employment relationship, an independent contractor arrangement, or an equity-holder or member-owner arrangement. The financial terms of the affiliation --- including whether Lakeview takes a percentage of the arbitrator\'s fees for matters administered through the center, whether Lakeview receives separate administrative fees for referred matters, or whether Lakeview\'s revenue model depends on maintaining referral relationships with law firms that regularly retain its affiliated neutrals --- are not publicly available. These financial arrangements may be relevant to evaluating the nature and extent of any economic interdependency between the arbitrator, Lakeview, and the referring law firms.

As discussed in Section III.6 above, Lakeview hosted the October 14, 2023 CLE event titled \"Arbitrating Complex IP Licensing Disputes,\" at which Judge Fairhaven and Mr. Hargrave served as co-panelists.

**4.2 Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute**

The Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute is a nonprofit professional organization focused on promoting ethical standards in alternative dispute resolution. The Institute provides programming, publications, and professional development opportunities for ADR practitioners, attorneys, corporate users of arbitration, and academic researchers in the fields of mediation, arbitration, and related dispute resolution processes.

The Institute\'s governance structure includes two principal bodies: a **Board of Trustees**, which serves as the governing body of the organization, and an **advisory board**, which provides substantive guidance on programming, ethics standards, and professional development initiatives.

The following affiliations with the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute are relevant to this matter:

> • **Board of Trustees:** Garrett T. Whitmore, Founder and Managing Partner of Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (the respondent in this arbitration) --- per IRS Form 990 filings for fiscal year 2023.
>
> • **Advisory Board:** Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.) (the arbitrator in this matter) --- since 2021, per her disclosure statement.
>
> • **Advisory Board:** Ellen Wu, Senior Partner, Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (head of the firm\'s national arbitration practice; involved in arbitrator selection strategy for this matter) --- per the arbitrator\'s disclosure statement.

The Institute is described in its public materials as a small nonprofit with limited staff and a focused mission centered on ADR ethics. Annual meetings and joint sessions between the Board of Trustees and the advisory board are held to coordinate programming and organizational strategy, per the Institute\'s publicly available bylaws and posted meeting minutes. The small size of the organization and the overlapping meeting structures increase the likelihood of direct personal interaction among members of both bodies.

Cross-references: See Section 1.1 (Garrett T. Whitmore), Section 2.1 (Ellen Wu), and Section III.3 (arbitrator\'s affiliation) above.

**4.3 Northbridge Wealth Advisors**

Northbridge Wealth Advisors is an investment management firm that manages the arbitrator\'s personal investment portfolio, as disclosed in the arbitrator\'s June 17, 2024 disclosure statement. In addition to managing individual client portfolios, Northbridge Wealth Advisors manages the **Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund**, a fund-of-funds product that allocates capital across a diversified portfolio of private equity, venture capital, and alternative investment strategies.

Per the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund\'s most recent public filing, dated March 2024, the fund holds a **2.3% limited partner interest in Whitmore Fund II LP**. Whitmore Fund II LP is a Delaware limited partnership managed by Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (the respondent in this arbitration) and is the fund that owns Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, the direct beneficiary of the disputed ThermalCore technology license. The potential chain of indirect financial interest between the arbitrator and Whitmore Fund II LP, through the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund, is discussed in detail in Section III.4 above.

Cross-references: See Section III.4 (arbitrator\'s investment interests) and Section 1.3 (Volaris) above.

result:
**AMERICAN ARBITRATION ASSOCIATION**

**COMMERCIAL ARBITRATION RULES AND MEDIATION PROCEDURES**

(Effective October 1, 2013; Amended and Effective September 1, 2022)

**Selected Excerpts**

Compiled for reference in AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217, *Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. v. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC*

**Note:** These excerpts are provided for counsel\'s convenience and do not constitute the complete AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules and Mediation Procedures. Parties and arbitrators should consult the full rules as published by the American Arbitration Association. Where a rule is excerpted in part, omitted portions are indicated by bracketed ellipses. Rule numbering and lettering correspond to the official AAA publication.

**[Rule R-1. Agreement of Parties]{.underline}**

**(a)** The parties shall be deemed to have made these Rules a part of their arbitration agreement whenever they have provided for arbitration by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules or have otherwise agreed to arbitration administered by the AAA in a commercial dispute. These Rules, together with any amendments thereto in effect at the time of the commencement of the arbitration, shall govern the arbitration proceeding.

**(b)** The parties may, by mutual written agreement, modify any provision of these Rules, except where these Rules expressly provide that a particular provision may not be modified by the parties. Any such modification shall be communicated in writing to the AAA and to the arbitrator.

**(c)** The AAA shall administer the arbitration in accordance with these Rules. These Rules shall be interpreted and applied by the AAA and by the arbitrator in a manner consistent with their purpose of providing a fair, efficient, and economical process for the resolution of disputes.

**[Rule R-12. Appointment from National Roster]{.underline}**

**(a)** If the parties have not agreed upon an arbitrator or a method for appointing an arbitrator, the AAA shall send simultaneously to each party an identical list of not fewer than five (5) names of persons drawn from the AAA National Roster of Arbitrators. Each name on the list shall be accompanied by a brief biographical summary and any disclosures then available.

**(b)** The parties may agree upon an arbitrator from the submitted list. If the parties are unable to agree upon an arbitrator within the time period specified by the AAA, each party shall strike from the list the names of any persons to whom that party objects, shall number the remaining names in order of preference, and shall return the list to the AAA within the time period specified.

**(c)** From among the persons who have been approved on both lists, and in accordance with the order of mutual preference as indicated by the parties, the AAA shall invite the acceptance of an arbitrator to serve. If no mutually acceptable arbitrator is identified through this process, or if the identified arbitrator is unable or unwilling to serve, the AAA shall have the authority to appoint an arbitrator from the National Roster without further submission of lists to the parties.

**(d)** The AAA shall notify all parties of the appointment of the arbitrator and shall confirm the appointment in writing.

**[Rule R-16. Disclosure and Challenge Procedure --- General]{.underline}**

Any person appointed or to be appointed as an arbitrator shall disclose to the AAA any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence, including any bias or any financial or personal interest in the result of the arbitration, or any past or present relationship with the parties, their representatives, or their counsel. The obligation to disclose under this Rule and Rule R-17 shall be construed broadly to further the goal of ensuring that arbitrators are and remain impartial and independent. The standard of \"justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence\" is intended to encompass all circumstances that a reasonable person would consider relevant in evaluating an arbitrator\'s ability to render a fair and unbiased determination.

**[Rule R-17. Disclosure]{.underline}**

**(a)** A person appointed as an arbitrator shall disclose to the AAA any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence. The obligation of disclosure extends to, but is not limited to, the following categories of information:

> **(i)** Any known direct or indirect financial or personal interest in the outcome of the arbitration, including any interest, whether legal or beneficial, in the subject matter of the dispute, in any party, or in any entity that may be affected by the result of the proceeding;
>
> **(ii)** Any known existing or past financial, business, professional, family, or social relationships with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, representatives, or witnesses that are likely to affect impartiality or that might reasonably create an appearance of partiality or bias. This includes, without limitation, relationships arising from employment, professional association, joint ventures, board memberships, academic collaboration, or regular social contact;
>
> **(iii)** Any prior or pending cases in which the arbitrator has been or is involved as arbitrator, counsel, expert, or in any other capacity, with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, or representatives. This obligation extends to all matters, whether administered by the AAA or by any other arbitral institution, and includes matters in which the arbitrator served in any neutral or non-neutral capacity;
>
> **(iv)** Any other circumstances that might cause a reasonable party to question the arbitrator\'s ability to render a fair and impartial determination. This catch-all provision is intended to ensure comprehensive disclosure and shall be interpreted broadly to encompass facts and circumstances not specifically enumerated in subparagraphs (i) through (iii) above, including but not limited to the arbitrator\'s prior publications, public statements, or expressed views on legal or factual issues relevant to the dispute.

**(b)** The obligation of disclosure is a continuing obligation throughout the arbitration proceeding. An arbitrator must disclose any new information or any change in circumstances that arises after the initial disclosure as soon as practicable. The arbitrator shall not await inquiry by a party or by the AAA before making any required supplemental disclosure.

**(c)** Upon receipt of disclosures from an arbitrator, the AAA shall communicate the disclosures to the parties promptly and in their entirety. If the arbitrator, at any stage during the arbitration, becomes aware of new circumstances that should be disclosed, the arbitrator shall promptly disclose such circumstances to the parties and to the AAA. The AAA shall transmit any supplemental disclosures to all parties within two (2) business days of receipt.

**(d)** The failure of an arbitrator to make complete and timely disclosures as required by this Rule may result in the arbitrator\'s removal in accordance with Rule R-18, and may constitute grounds for vacatur of any resulting award under applicable law, including but not limited to Section 10 of the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. § 10). The AAA may, in its discretion, bring any disclosure deficiency to the attention of the arbitrator and request supplemental disclosure before acting on any challenge.

**[Rule R-18. Disqualification of Arbitrator; Challenge Procedure]{.underline}**

**(a)** Any party may challenge the appointment of an arbitrator on grounds that circumstances exist that give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence. A challenge must be made by written notice to the AAA and to the other party, stating with specificity the grounds for the challenge, within **seven (7) calendar days** after (i) the appointment of the challenged arbitrator has been communicated to the challenging party, or (ii) the circumstances giving rise to the challenge became known or should reasonably have become known to the challenging party, whichever is later.

**(b)** Upon receipt of a challenge, the AAA shall communicate the challenge to the other party or parties, who may respond in writing within seven (7) calendar days of receipt of the challenge. The challenged arbitrator shall also be notified and may submit a written response to the AAA within the same period. The AAA may, in its discretion, extend the response period for good cause shown.

**(c)** If the other party agrees to the challenge, or the challenged arbitrator voluntarily withdraws from the proceeding, a replacement arbitrator shall be appointed in accordance with Rule R-19. The withdrawal of an arbitrator or the agreement of a party to a challenge shall not imply acceptance of the validity of the grounds stated in the challenge.
